name: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.

profile.description: Cleveland-Cliffs is the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America, with approximately 16.5-17 million net tons of annual steelmaking capacity. Through four major acquisitions (AK Steel, ArcelorMittal USA, Ferrous Processing & Trading, and Stelco), the company transformed from an iron ore miner into a vertically integrated steel and mining company serving automotive, infrastructure, and manufacturing markets. Headquartered in Cleveland, Ohio.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (1/3): Cleveland-Cliffs executed four transformative acquisitions in four years (AK Steel, ArcelorMittal USA, FPT, Stelco) that reinvented the company from an iron ore miner to the largest North American flat-rolled steel producer [1]. However, the cancelled $500M green steel project in 2025 and revenue declining from $23.0B (2022) to $18.6B (2025) indicate the integrated model has not yet delivered sustained value. AI Deployment (1/2): CLF formalized an AI partnership with Palantir in April 2026, with CEO Goncalves stating Palantir's platform allows the company to 'solve problems in ways humans simply cannot in integrated steelmaking' [2]. The company is pivoting from manually driven operational procedures to AI-assisted models for process optimization and quality control. Market Share (1/2): Largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America with dominant automotive steel supply positions, but market share is not growing and revenue is declining [1]. Product Pipeline (0/2): No next-generation products with specific timelines; the POSCO partnership for EV-grade advanced high-strength steel remains unconfirmed. R&D Intensity (1/1): Operates a Research and Innovation Center with technology group, and the Palantir AI investment signals above-average tech commitment for the steel industry [2].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): CEO Lourenco Goncalves has articulated a clear vision of rebuilding American steel manufacturing and domestic supply chain security — a purpose that connects the company's work to national industrial policy and community economic vitality [2]. The mission is specific and genuine but more political than visionary, and it has not been tested through leadership transitions. Community Impact (2/2): Cleveland-Cliffs is the largest employer in multiple steel towns across the Midwest (Cleveland, Burns Harbor, Weirton, Dearborn), supporting middle-class union jobs with best-in-class pay in communities that have few alternative employers [3]. The company's 25% GHG reduction commitment by 2030 and HBI plant investment demonstrate genuine community and environmental stewardship. Employee Retention (1/2): The company employs ~25,000 workers under unionized contracts (USW), providing job stability and competitive wages. Goncalves has maintained a consistent leadership team through four major acquisitions. However, heavy industry turnover is inherently challenging, and no specific tenure data is available. Insider Buying (0/2): No evidence of meaningful net insider buying by Goncalves or other executives in the last 12 months. Goncalves has been a net seller of shares.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. FY2025 was financially catastrophic — GAAP net loss of $1.4B ($2.91/share), Adjusted EBITDA of only $37M (down from $773M in 2024), and negative operating cash flow of $436M [1]. D/E of 129.3% is elevated from acquisition financing, with $7.8B total debt against only $45M cash. ROE is deeply negative at -18.6% and profit margin is -6.4%. Forward PEG of 0.43 is attractive, reflecting the expected 2026 earnings recovery. Q1 2026 showed improvement with $4.9B revenue (+6.3% YoY), and management expects $500M EBITDA improvement from slab contract termination plus $10/ton unit cost reductions [1]. However, 3-year revenue CAGR of -6.8% and current negative profitability score near floor on most metrics.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): Steel manufacturing is fundamentally a physical-world, capital-intensive industry where AI structurally cannot displace the core activity of converting iron ore into steel via blast furnaces, electric arc furnaces, or direct reduction plants [1]. Cleveland-Cliffs operates complex physical infrastructure — blast furnaces, rolling mills, coke batteries, pellet plants — that requires massive capital investment and decades of metallurgical expertise that AI cannot replicate. Analysts do not cite AI as a displacement risk for CLF's business. Furthermore, AI-driven demand for data centers requires steel for construction, potentially increasing demand for CLF's products. The primary risks are tariffs, automotive demand cycles, and Chinese overcapacity — not AI substitution. AI Opportunity (1/1): The formalized Palantir AI partnership (April 2026) demonstrates measurable AI revenue contribution on the operational side, with Goncalves citing AI as enabling solutions for 'complex issues in integrated steelmaking' [2]. AI is deployed for process optimization, predictive maintenance, and quality control across the company's manufacturing footprint.

infinite_game.summary: CEO Goncalves has demonstrated genuine courage through a decade of bold acquisitions that transformed Cleveland-Cliffs from a niche iron ore company into the dominant U.S. flat-rolled steel producer. His articulated cause of rebuilding American steel manufacturing is authentic and has been consistently pursued. However, his combative public stance toward competitors (US Steel), reliance on government tariff protection, and cancellation of the green steel project in favor of financial pragmatism reflect a mixed infinite/finite mindset.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (1/3): Goncalves's articulated cause — rebuilding American steel manufacturing and domestic supply chain security — is genuine, service-oriented (it benefits communities and the national industrial base), and has been consistently pursued through a decade of acquisitions [2]. His advocacy for steel tariffs and domestic procurement rules reflects strategic conviction. However, the cause is political in framing rather than visionary, and 'make American steel great again' reads as finite-game competitive positioning rather than an infinite Just Cause that transcends any single company.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Goncalves has maintained a core leadership team through four major acquisitions without catastrophic cultural failures — integrating AK Steel, ArcelorMittal USA, FPT, and Stelco required trust between diverse organizational cultures [2]. Board refreshment in 2026 suggests governance dynamics are evolving constructively. Union relationships (USW) provide structured labor-management trust, though heavy industry inherently limits the agility Sinek prizes. Employee headcount has declined from ~30,000 to ~25,000, reflecting some organizational stress.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Goncalves's public language toward US Steel and foreign steel producers is combative — focused on winning market share, blocking competition through tariffs, and disparaging competitor management [2]. His war of words during the US Steel acquisition attempt reflects finite competitive thinking. The company's competitive strategy centers on political advocacy (tariffs) and M&A rather than self-improvement inspired by rivals' strengths.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The transformation from iron ore miner to integrated steelmaker represents meaningful strategic evolution — CLF fundamentally changed what it does through four acquisitions [1]. The HBI production capability and Palantir AI partnership represent genuine adaptation. However, the cancelled green hydrogen project and return to conventional production when cost pressures arrived suggest the flexibility has limits. The company has not abandoned any core business — it has only added to its steel empire.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Goncalves's four acquisitions in four years — including ArcelorMittal USA for $1.4B during COVID — required extraordinary financial courage and long-term conviction [1]. Maintaining CapEx commitment of ~$700M through a steel downcycle (FY2025 net loss of $1.4B) while competitors cut demonstrates willingness to sacrifice near-term results for long-term capability. However, the green steel project cancellation suggests pragmatism over principle when costs became uncomfortable.

investment_thesis: Cleveland-Cliffs is a turnaround play with identifiable 2026 catalysts. The slab supply contract termination delivers an estimated $500M annual EBITDA benefit, management guides to $10/net ton cost reductions, and Q1 2026 revenue of $4.9B (+6.3% YoY) suggests the operational trough has passed [1]. At forward PEG of 0.43, the market is pricing in significant earnings recovery — forward EPS of $0.44 implies a return to profitability. The Palantir AI partnership provides operational efficiency upside, tariff protections support domestic steel pricing, and the company's dominant automotive OEM relationships (multi-year fixed-price contracts) provide revenue visibility [2]. Stock has rallied 37% from its 50-day SMA of $9.90 to $13.60, reflecting improving sentiment.
thesis_continued: The bear case is formidable. FY2025 financial performance was catastrophic — $1.4B GAAP net loss, $37M Adjusted EBITDA, and negative operating cash flow of $436M [1]. The balance sheet carries $7.8B in total debt against only $45M cash, with D/E of 129% leaving minimal flexibility if recovery lags. Revenue has declined from $23.0B (2022) to $18.6B (2025), a 3-year CAGR of -6.8%, and there is no dividend to compensate shareholders during the recovery period. Automotive exposure (~30% of revenue) faces EV transition uncertainty that could suppress steel consumption for body-in-white applications. Forward P/E of 31x on extremely depressed earnings makes valuation misleading — the stock is priced for a recovery that requires every catalyst to materialize simultaneously [4]. Chinese steel overcapacity and global price competition could erode domestic pricing power despite tariffs.
key_catalysts: Slab contract termination delivering estimated $500M annual EBITDA benefit in FY2026, representing the single largest financial improvement lever and potentially returning the company to positive net income
Palantir AI partnership driving measurable operational efficiency gains across integrated steelmaking, with potential to reduce costs beyond the guided $10/net ton target
U.S. steel tariff protections maintaining domestic pricing floor while automotive OEM demand recovers, supporting both volume (16.5-17.0M net tons guided) and pricing leverage
key_risks: Balance sheet stress — $7.8B total debt against $45M cash with D/E of 129% and negative operating cash flow leaves no margin of safety if the 2026 recovery is slower than guided, potentially forcing dilutive financing

Automotive production remaining weak through 2026 as OEMs manage the EV transition and inventory adjustments — CLF derives ~30% of revenues from automotive, and any prolonged softness directly impacts shipment volumes
Revenue decline persisting — 3-year revenue CAGR of -6.8% reflects structural headwinds (Chinese overcapacity, global price competition, demand shifts) that tariff protection alone cannot offset
